metadata.title: Why One Zero Beats Four Nines
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Reallocation Engine candidate 07. Source chapter: books/the-reallocation-engine/chapters/11-the-bayesian-role-scorer.md (the votes-vs-gates arithmetic only — multipliers annihilate where addends dilute).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most; a score pipeline with two inline valves as the hero object
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, dusty navy = the flow / a healthy factor / Apply, crimson = the closed gate / the zero / Skip, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: dusty navy #3D5A80 = the flow / healthy factor / Apply; crimson #BF3339 = the closed valve / the zero / Skip; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO full composite formula or weight VALUES (the 0.35/0.30 weights and the exact expression never appear), NO Bayesian terminology (never say 'Bayesian', 'prior', 'posterior'), NO threshold-tuning (a cutoff LINE appears but no number and no adjusting it), NO Eightfold-lawsuit aside, NO visa-law specifics (framed universally: 'can they hire you', 'can you start in time' — no OPT/H-1B/LCA). The gate-vs-vote arithmetic contrast is the ENTIRE film. From ch.11: fit & sponsorship are VOTES (addends, proportional); liveness & timeline are GATES (multipliers that drive the composite toward zero); 'no amount of fit or sponsorship can carry a role through a gate that is closed'; 'a perfect role at a sponsoring company is worth nothing if the posting is fiction or you cannot start in time.' On-screen contrast numbers (0.9s and a 0; averaged ~0.68 vs multiplied 0) are ILLUSTRATIVE, not the scorer's real weights — labeled as such.

beats.narration_text: A job can score ninety-five percent on everything that matters, and still be worth exactly zero. Here's the arithmetic that tells you which.

card.copy: Why one zero beats four nines

beats.narration_text: Here's a role that looks perfect. Great company, strong match, everything you'd want. The score comes back high. You're ready to apply.
beats.scene_description: a polished job posting at a famous-looking company pinned to the newsprint ground, a big green 'match: 95%' badge; slow push-in on the badge
beats.image_prompt: printed screenshot of a polished corporate job posting at a prestigious company, a prominent green '95% match' badge, clean UI, pinned like a clipping to aged newsprint, desaturated flat print reproduction, editorial collage

beats.narration_text: A score like this is built from parts. Some are votes — how well you fit, whether they can even hire you. They add up. More is better.

production_viz.label: votes add up
production_viz.mechanic: two navy vote bars ('fit', 'can they hire you') grow and merge into a single navy flow arrow heading right; a plus sign between them
production_viz.note: votes = addends. No weight values shown (card exclusion).

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two votes sum into one flow

beats.narration_text: But two of the parts aren't votes at all. They're gates. Is the job actually real? Can you start in time? Each is a valve the whole flow must pass through.

production_viz.label: two valves in the line
production_viz.mechanic: the navy flow arrow runs right and passes through two inline valve rings labelled 'is it real?' and 'can you start?'; both open (navy) so the flow continues to an 'Apply' node
production_viz.note: gates = liveness, timeline. Framed universally (no visa-law).

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the flow must pass two valves

viz.label: gates are valves in the line

beats.narration_text: And a gate doesn't subtract — it multiplies. Close one valve, the start date you can't meet, and the flow doesn't shrink. It collapses to zero.
beats.scene_description: the strong navy flow reaches the second valve; the valve slams crimson-shut; the flow downstream collapses from full to a flat zero; a crimson 'x 0' tag; the 'Apply' node flips to 'Skip'

production_viz.label: close a valve → collapse to zero
production_viz.mechanic: full navy flow hits the 'can you start?' valve; valve turns crimson and shuts; downstream flow drops to a flat zero line; crimson 'x0' stamp; node flips Apply -> Skip
production_viz.note: the collapse move — the heart of the film. Multiply, not subtract.

beats.new_visual_element: [COMPOSITE] valve shuts → flow collapses to zero

beats.narration_text: Here's the trap. Average everything instead — treat that zero as just one low number among high ones — and it barely dents the score. Still looks like apply.

production_viz.label: averaging hides the zero
production_viz.mechanic: four tall navy bars (~0.9) and one crimson zero bar; an 'average' arrow collapses them into one bar that stays high (~0.68), sitting ABOVE a dashed 'apply line'; label 'still looks like apply'
production_viz.note: ILLUSTRATIVE numbers (0.9s + 0 -> avg ~0.68). Not the scorer's real weights.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] average of nines and a zero stays high

beats.narration_text: Same zero, two different fates. Averaged, it hides, and the score says go. Multiplied, it dominates, and the score says stop.
beats.scene_description: two result bars side by side for the same inputs: left 'averaged' bar high above the apply line (crimson ring — 'hides'), right 'multiplied' bar flat at zero (navy — 'honest'); labels 'go' vs 'stop'

production_viz.label: averaged vs multiplied — same zero
production_viz.mechanic: left bar tall above the dashed apply line, tagged 'averaged: go' with a crimson ring (the lie); right bar flat zero, tagged 'multiplied: stop' in navy (the truth)
production_viz.note: the direct contrast — same inputs, opposite verdicts

annotation.target: the averaged bar (the one that hides the zero)

beats.narration_text: Because multiply admits what average hides. A perfect job you can't start is worth nothing at all.

document.quote: A job you can't start is worth nothing.
document.attribution: — what the gate knows and the average forgets

beats.scene_description: the honest statement as a large serif quote card; gold highlighter sweeps 'nothing' as it is spoken
beats.new_visual_element: [QUOTE] a job you can't start is worth nothing

beats.narration_text: This is how people burn weeks applying to jobs the calendar already killed. The average told them go. The gate would have said stop.
beats.scene_description: a wall calendar with a passed deadline crossed in crimson; application envelopes fly toward a closed crimson door and pile up unopened; label 'the calendar already closed'

production_viz.label: jobs the calendar already killed
production_viz.mechanic: a calendar page with a crimson X on a passed date; a stack of navy envelopes flies at a shut crimson door and piles unopened; serif label 'already closed'
production_viz.note: the everyday cost of averaging a gate

beats.new_visual_element: [COMPOSITE] applications pile at a door the calendar shut

beats.narration_text: So never average a gate. A job earns your time only if it clears every valve. One stays shut, and the answer is zero, however great the rest.

production_viz.label: clears every valve, or it's zero
production_viz.mechanic: two mini pipelines: top — both valves open, navy flow reaches 'Apply'; bottom — one valve shut crimson, flow flat at zero reaching 'Skip'; label 'every valve, or zero'
production_viz.note: the rule, stated as two pipelines

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] all valves open = Apply; one shut = Skip

viz.label: clears every gate or it's zero

beats.narration_text: Nines and a zero. Multiply, and the zero always wins.

card.copy: Nines and a zero — multiply, and zero wins.
